---
title: "Week 4 — Clinical Data Types"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Define continuous, ordinal, nominal, and time-to-event data types
- Classify clinical variables by their appropriate data type
- Explain why data type determines the correct statistical analysis
- Create and use factors in R for categorical data
- Identify when a number is actually a category (and vice versa)
- Choose appropriate summary statistics for each data type

## Why Data Types Matter

In Week 3, you learned to structure data. This week you learn to **classify** it. The same tidy table can lead to completely wrong conclusions if you analyze a categorical variable as continuous, or a continuous variable as categorical.

**The data type determines:**
- Which summary statistics are valid (mean vs. median vs. mode)
- Which statistical tests apply (t-test vs. chi-square vs. Kaplan-Meier)
- Which visualizations make sense (histogram vs. bar chart vs. survival curve)

Misclassification is a common source of clinical research errors. This week builds the defense.

## Core Concepts

### Continuous Data

**Definition:** Numeric values where differences are meaningful and ratios may be meaningful. Can (in theory) take any value in a range.

**Clinical examples:**
- Mean arterial pressure (MAP) — 65, 72, 68, 75 mmHg
- Heart rate — 72, 85, 90 bpm
- Temperature — 36.5, 37.2, 38.0 °C
- Lab values — creatinine 1.2, 1.5, 2.0 mg/dL

**R type:** `numeric` (double or integer)

**Appropriate summaries:** Mean, standard deviation, median, IQR, range

**Inappropriate summaries:** Mode (usually), frequency table

```r
# Continuous variable
map <- c(65, 72, 68, 75, 82, 70, 68, 74)
mean(map)
sd(map)
median(map)
```

### Ordinal Data

**Definition:** Categorical with a clear order, but differences between values are not necessarily equal or meaningful.

**Clinical examples:**
- ASA physical status — I, II, III, IV, V, VI (VI = declared brain dead for organ donation)
- NYHA heart failure class — I, II, III, IV
- Pain score — 0, 1, 2, ..., 10
- GCS motor response — 1 (none) to 6 (obeys commands)

**R type:** `ordered factor`

**Key insight:** The difference between ASA I and II is not the same as between ASA IV and V. You cannot say "ASA IV is twice as sick as ASA II."

**Appropriate summaries:** Median, mode, frequency table, proportion in each category

**Inappropriate summaries:** Mean (mathematically possible but clinically meaningless)

```r
# Ordinal variable — ASA class
asa <- ordered(c("II", "III", "II", "IV", "III", "II"), 
               levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"))
table(asa)
median(asa)  # Returns "III" — the middle category
```

### Nominal Data

**Definition:** Categories without any natural order.

**Clinical examples:**
- Sex — male, female
- Blood type — A, B, AB, O
- Diagnosis code — appendicitis, cholecystitis, trauma
- Anesthetic technique — general, spinal, epidural, local
- Hospital site — Site A, Site B, Site C

**R type:** `factor` (unordered) or `character`

**Critical trap:** Numbers assigned to categories (1 = male, 2 = female) are **not numeric.** Computing a mean sex of 1.4 is nonsense.

**Appropriate summaries:** Frequency table, mode, proportions

**Inappropriate summaries:** Mean, median, standard deviation, min, max

```r
# Nominal variable — Sex
sex <- factor(c("male", "female", "female", "male", "male"))
table(sex)
prop.table(table(sex))
```

### Time-to-Event Data (Survival Data)

**Definition:** Time from a defined start point until an event occurs — **with censoring.**

**Clinical examples:**
- Time from ICU admission to death or discharge
- Time from surgery to first postoperative complication
- Time from intubation to extubation

**The censoring problem:** At time of analysis, some patients have not yet had the event (still in ICU, still alive). Their time is **censored** — we know they survived at least 5 days, but not their final time.

**R type:** Requires two variables:
- `time` — time observed
- `status` — 1 = event occurred, 0 = censored

**Why special handling:** Simply excluding censored patients biases results (only counting patients who had the event quickly). Simply using the observed time underestimates true time-to-event.

**Appropriate analysis:** Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox regression, log-rank test

**Inappropriate analysis:** t-test on observed times, mean time-to-event

```r
# Time-to-event data structure (not analysis — that comes in Week 13)
icu_time <- data.frame(
  patient_id = c(101, 102, 103, 104),
  days_in_icu = c(5, 12, 8, 3),
  died_in_icu = c(0, 1, 0, 1)  # 0 = censored (discharged alive), 1 = event (died)
)
# Patient 101 and 103 are censored — we know they survived ICU, 
# but not their total survival time
```

## Factors in R

Factors are R's way of storing categorical data. They look like text but store numbers internally.

### Creating Factors

```r
# Nominal factor
sex <- factor(c("male", "female", "female", "male"))

# Ordinal factor (explicit order)
asa <- ordered(c("II", "III", "II", "IV"), 
               levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV", "V"))

# Check the levels
levels(sex)
levels(asa)
```

### Common Factor Traps

**Trap 1: Character vectors that look like numbers**

```r
# Excel export problem
patient_ids <- c("101", "102", "103")  # Character!
patient_ids_numeric <- as.numeric(patient_ids)  # Convert if needed
```

**Trap 2: Numeric codes without labels**

```r
# Bad: What does 2 mean?
sex_numeric <- c(1, 2, 2, 1)  # 1 = male? 2 = female?

# Good: Explicit factor
sex_factor <- factor(c("male", "female", "female", "male"))
```

**Trap 3: Factors that are secretly ordered**

```r
# Wrong — treated as nominal
pain_wrong <- factor(c(0, 2, 5, 3, 8))

# Right — treated as ordinal
pain_right <- ordered(c(0, 2, 5, 3, 8), levels = 0:10)
```

## Exercise

### Part 1: Classify Clinical Variables

For each variable below, identify:
1. Data type (continuous, ordinal, nominal, time-to-event)
2. Appropriate R storage type (numeric, factor, ordered, or two-column structure)
3. One appropriate summary statistic
4. One inappropriate summary statistic (that would be wrong to use)

| Variable | Type | R Type | Appropriate | Inappropriate |
|----------|------|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Heart rate (bpm) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| ASA class (I-VI) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| Blood type (A/B/AB/O) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| ICU length of stay (days, with some patients still in ICU) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| GCS total score (3-15) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| GCS motor component (1-6) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| Propofol dose (mg) | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| Mechanical ventilation (yes/no) | ? | ? | ? | ? |

Fill in the table in your Quarto document with explanations.

### Part 2: Create Factors in R

Create the following variables in R, ensuring correct types:

```r
# 1. ASA classes for 6 patients: II, III, II, IV, III, II
asa <- ordered(c("II", "III", "II", "IV", "III", "II"), 
               levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"))

# 2. Sex for the same 6 patients: male, female, female, male, male, female
sex <- factor(c("male", "female", "female", "male", "male", "female"))

# 3. MAP readings (continuous): 72, 68, 75, 82, 70, 68
map <- c(72, 68, 75, 82, 70, 68)

# Verify with str() — "structure" function
str(asa)
str(sex)
str(map)
```

### Part 3: SOFA Score Component Classification

The SOFA (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) score has 6 components. Each is scored 0-4, but the inputs come from different data types.

**Component details:**

| Component | Input Variable | SOFA Score |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Respiratory | PaO2/FiO2 ratio (mmHg) | 0: ≥400, 1: <400, 2: <300, 3: <200, 4: <100 |
| Coagulation | Platelet count (×10³/μL) | 0: ≥150, 1: <150, 2: <100, 3: <50, 4: <20 |
| Liver | Bilirubin (mg/dL) | 0: <1.2, 1: 1.2-1.9, 2: 2.0-5.9, 3: 6.0-11.9, 4: ≥12 |
| Cardiovascular | MAP (mmHg) or vasopressor use | 0: MAP ≥70, 1: MAP <70, 2-4: dopamine/norepinephrine doses |
| Neurological | GCS score | 0: 15, 1: 13-14, 2: 10-12, 3: 6-9, 4: <6 |
| Renal | Creatinine (mg/dL) or urine output | 0: <1.2, 1: 1.2-1.9, 2: 2.0-3.4, 3: 3.5-4.9, 4: ≥5 |

**Your task:**

For the **input variables** (PaO2/FiO2, platelets, bilirubin, MAP, GCS, creatinine), classify each:
1. Continuous, ordinal, or nominal?
2. What R type should store the raw measurement?
3. Why is the conversion to SOFA 0-4 score a **lossy transformation**? (What information is lost?)
4. The SOFA 0-4 score itself — is this ordinal or interval? Can you say "SOFA 3 is three times worse than SOFA 1"?

Write your answers in a Quarto document with justification for each.

### Part 4: Common Errors in Published Papers

Below are real (anonymized) errors from published anesthesia papers. Identify the data type error:

**Error A:** "The mean ASA class was 2.4 (SD 0.8)"

**Error B:** "We compared time to extubation between groups using a t-test"

**Error C:** "Sex was coded as 1 = male, 2 = female, and entered as a continuous predictor in the regression"

**Error D:** "The average pain score decreased from 8.2 to 4.1, a 50% reduction"

For each:
1. What is wrong?
2. What should have been done instead?
3. What is the clinical consequence of the error?

### Part 5: Reflection

Write 2–3 sentences:

> Why does data type classification matter more in clinical research than in many other fields? Hint: think about composite scores, time-to-event outcomes, and the difference between "counting" and "measuring."

---

## Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Prevent |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| "I computed the mean of my ordinal pain scores" | Pain scores are numbers, so mean seems natural | Remember: 8/10 pain is not "twice" 4/10 pain. Report median and IQR. |
| "My categorical variable has numbers, so I treated it as continuous" | Numeric codes look numeric | Use `factor()` or `ordered()` explicitly. Check with `str()`. |
| "I excluded patients still in the ICU from my length-of-stay analysis" | Censored data feels incomplete | Time-to-event requires survival analysis. Exclusion biases toward sicker patients. |
| "My factor levels are in alphabetical order, not clinical order" | R defaults to alphabetical | Use `levels = c(...)` to specify order explicitly. |
| "I reported mean ± SD for GCS" | GCS is numeric 3-15 | GCS is ordinal. A mean GCS of 10.5 is clinically meaningless. Report median or proportions in each category. |

## Verification

Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

- [ ] Identify continuous, ordinal, nominal, and time-to-event data in clinical context
- [ ] Explain why data type determines appropriate statistical analysis
- [ ] Create factors and ordered factors in R
- [ ] Classify SOFA components correctly and explain why the conversion is lossy
- [ ] Identify statistical errors in published papers related to data type misclassification
- [ ] Push your Quarto document to GitHub

## Resources

- **R for Data Science (2e):** [r4ds.hadley.nz/factors.html](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/factors.html) — Factors chapter
- **SOFA Score:** Vincent, et al. (1996). "The SOFA score to describe organ dysfunction/failure." *Intensive Care Medicine*
- **ASA Physical Status:** [ASA House of Delegates, 2020](https://www.asahq.org/standards-and-practice-parameters/statement-on-asa-physical-status-classification-system)
- **Survival Analysis in R:** [therinspark.com/survival.html](https://therinspark.com/survival.html) — For Week 13 prep

---

[← Back to Basic Tier](../index.qmd)
